Name: geometric_subspace_routing_for_mixed_precision
Title: Geometric Subspace Routing for Training-Free Mixed Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging geometric subspace diagnostics to identify layers with distinct curvature properties, we can deterministically allocate mixed precision bit-widths to maximize model efficiency without requiring training or calibration data.
Related Work: Existing training-free mixed precision methods, such as EMQ and SNIP, rely on heuristic proxies or calibration data to allocate bit-widths. These approaches often lack transparency and fail to account for the geometric properties of the model's loss landscape. Our proposal differs by using curvature-aware geometric subspace diagnostics to inform bit-width allocation, providing a deterministic, mathematically grounded alternative.
Abstract: Mixed precision quantization (MPQ) offers a promising approach to reduce the computational and memory footprint of deep neural networks. However, existing training-free methods often rely on heuristic proxies or calibration data, which can be inefficient and lack transparency. We propose a novel framework that leverages geometric subspace diagnostics to identify layers with distinct curvature properties, enabling deterministic bit-width allocation without the need for training or calibration data. Our approach quantifies gradient covariance decay, Hessian conditioning, and feature entanglement to formalize a training-free routing protocol. This protocol replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii, PAC-Bayes generalization bounds, and explicit condition number constraints under precision switching. We validate our approach on ImageNet with ResNet and MobileNet architectures, demonstrating superior efficiency and accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate gradient covariance decay across layers to identify high-variance regions that benefit from higher precision.
Experiment 2: Measure Hessian conditioning to determine layers with poor curvature properties, which may require higher bit-widths.
Experiment 3: Quantify feature entanglement to isolate layers where precision reduction could lead to significant information loss.
Experiment 4: Compare our geometric subspace routing approach against state-of-the-art methods (e.g., EMQ, SNIP) on ImageNet using ResNet and MobileNet architectures.
Experiment 5: Analyze the computational overhead of our diagnostic pipeline to ensure feasibility on single-GPU setups.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The geometric subspace diagnostics may introduce computational overhead, although we aim to keep this minimal.
The approach assumes that curvature properties are consistent across different datasets, which may not hold in all cases.
The method relies on deterministic proxies, which may not capture all nuances of model behavior.
